narrative_blocks.header: Only son, present at sister's death
narrative_blocks.body: The only son of Edward Hooper La Farge and Anne Woolsey La Farge among seven children — six sisters. Resided Washington DC; present with his wife at his sister Elizabeth's January 2026 death in Quakertown PA. Carries the male La Farge line forward into the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Sole male heir of his La Farge line
narrative_blocks.body: As the only son among Anne Woolsey La Farge's seven children, he carries forward the male La Farge surname — descended from 19th-century American Symbolist painter John La Farge through his grandfather Bancel La Farge. Resided Washington DC at his mother's 2005 death and was present with his wife at his sister Elizabeth's January 2026 Quakertown PA death — the most recent documented Hooker descendant family event in this tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Sole male heir of his La Farge line
narrative_blocks.body: As the only son among Anne Woolsey La Farge's seven children, he carries forward the male La Farge surname — descended from 19th-century American Symbolist painter John La Farge through his grandfather Bancel La Farge. Resided Washington DC at his mother's 2005 death and was present with his wife at his sister Elizabeth's January 2026 Quakertown PA death — the most recent documented Hooker descendant family event in this tree.

burial.plot_notes: Saint Columba Catholic Cemetery, Middletown RI — with father Bancel + mother Mabel + grandfather John

marriages.officiant: Rev. John La Farge S.J. (the bridegroom's uncle, New York City)

career.organization: Nicholas Roosevelt, U.S. Ambassador to Hungary
career.notes: Ambassador appointed by President Franklin Delano Roosevelt.
30 years as art instructor at RISD.

narrative_blocks.header: RISD art professor for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Grandson of the artist John La Farge and son of muralist Bancel La Farge, he graduated Choate then attended Harvard. Before teaching he served as private secretary to Nicholas Roosevelt — FDR's Ambassador to Hungary — then taught art at the Rhode Island School of Design for approximately thirty years (c.1944-1974).

narrative_blocks.header: Third generation in a New York art dynasty
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather John La Farge pioneered American mural painting and stained glass; his father Bancel La Farge designed religious mosaics and altarpieces; he himself taught art at RISD for thirty years. The three generations spanned from the founding of the modern American mural movement at Trinity Church Boston 1878 to the post-war art school era of the 1970s.

sources.label: Smithsonian Archives of American Art — Edward H. La Farge Papers

marriages.spouse_name: Edward Hooper LaFarge (1901-1981), artist grandson of John LaFarge; RISD professor 30 yrs; m. 12 Jun 1935 Foxon East Haven CT; 7 children — Edward T, Natalie Goss, Anne Culman, Patricia Knopp, Elizabeth LaFarge, Margaret Pinsky, Catherine England

burial.plot_notes: Saint Columba Catholic Cemetery, Middletown RI

notable.notable_blurb: mural painter and decorative artist known for religious

parents.mother_name: Margaret Mason Perry LaFarge — daughter of Christopher Grant Perry (Brown 1830 + UPenn MD 1837) and Frances Sergeant Perry of Newport RI; great-granddaughter of Commodore Oliver Hazard Perry

education.school_name: University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine
Yale School of the Fine Arts

career.notes: Assisted his father at the studio for 10 years.
12 years in Europe with family — studied painting/drawing in Paris; conducted extensive research on Byzantine mosaics.

Returned to U.S. 1915. Designed altarpieces, stained glass, mosaic decorations, and wall paintings for churches and colleges. Federal Emergency Relief Administration commissioned New Haven Public Library lunettes 1934 (completed under Federal Arts Project).
Instrumental in starting the movement to obtain the Sleeping Giant as a Connecticut State Park. VP 1924-1934, President 1934-1938.

career.organization: Liturgical Arts Society of New York City

landmarks.blurb: VP of Sleeping Giant Park Association 1924-1934; President 1934-1938. Park established 1933.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve years studying mosaics across Europe
narrative_blocks.body: After 10 years assisting his father John La Farge in the New York studio (1893-1903), he moved his family to Paris and then Lausanne for twelve years (1903-15). He studied painting and drawing in Paris and conducted extensive research on Byzantine mosaics that informed his lifelong religious art.

narrative_blocks.header: Religious art at Trinity Washington and St. Aedan's
narrative_blocks.body: His best-known works include 'The Coronation of the Virgin' in Trinity College Chapel Washington (covering the large semi-dome over the altar, inspired by Dante's Earthly Paradise) and 'The Transfiguration' in St. Aedan's Church New Haven. He also designed twelve windows for the chapels in the Cathedral of St. Paul in Minnesota.

narrative_blocks.header: Started the Sleeping Giant State Park movement
narrative_blocks.body: From Edgehill in Mount Carmel CT he was instrumental in starting the movement to obtain the Sleeping Giant as a Connecticut State Park; he served as vice-president of the Sleeping Giant Park Association from its 1924 organization until 1934, then as president 1934-1938.

sources.label: Yale 1938-1939 Obituary Record of Graduates pp. 294-295

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Forest Service forester, WWI Major in the

career.notes: WWI commission as Major. The 10th Engineers were organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces; his duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use.
Post-WWI returned to CT as consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer; worked with national forestry organizations and conservation groups.

military_service.notes: Forestry timber procurement for AEF in France

narrative_blocks.header: WWI Major procuring timber in France for AEF
narrative_blocks.body: In 1917 he was commissioned Major in the 10th Engineers (Forestry) — a unit organized to produce timber in France for the American Expeditionary Forces. His duties included purchasing standing timber for manufacture into AEF use; he served until 1919 then returned to Connecticut as a consulting forester and Yale forestry school lecturer.

narrative_blocks.header: Author of American Forest Regulation textbook
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale forestry lecturer and consulting forester, he wrote 'American Forest Regulation' along with other books and articles on forestry and forest regulation. His work bridged the practical forestry of the U.S. Forest Service and the academic forestry program Yale pioneered.

narrative_blocks.header: Mount Woolsey named for him after death
narrative_blocks.body: He died by self-inflicted gunshot on 10 July 1933 at fifty-three. Days later, his daughter Betty Woolsey was on a climbing party in Wyoming's Bighorn Mountains; on hearing of his death she left for New Haven, and the remaining four climbers named the unclimbed peak they then summited Mount Woolsey in his memory. The name was formally approved in 1961.
